lessons.title: Spreadsheet warm-up — formulae, relative and absolute
lessons.objectives:
I can name the parts of a spreadsheet (cell, column, row)
I can use a formula to work things out
I can explain the difference between a relative and an absolute formula
I can format money cells as currency
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: formula · relative · absolute · ADD · SUM · MULTIPLY. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the RSC voting spreadsheet open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–15) Starter — name the parts. Pupils label a column, a row and a cell on the voting spreadsheet (drag the words), then choose the right meaning.
   • Support: matching word→meaning.  • Core: what does a formula start with / which row is B10.  • Challenge: spot the wrong currency formatting in column C.
2. (15–25) I-do → we-do — a formula starts with =. Read formulae in cell order: “E9 equals D9 times B4”. Likely error: saying the numbers not the cells → fix-words “say the cell, not the number.”
3. (25–40) Relative vs absolute. Demo: drag =D9*B4 down and watch it break, then lock it as =D9*$B$4. Pupils complete the activity worksheet (Support word-choice, Core write the locked formula + fill-blank $, Challenge the profit formula).
4. (40–50) You-do — build the model. Add the formulae, total a column with SUM, format money cells as currency, then screenshot onto the activity worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not type it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil shows one formula that worked.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: The RSC Live event — COUNTIF, formatting and data validation
lessons.objectives:
I can calculate discounted prices with a formula
I can use COUNTIF to count sold seats
I can use data validation to make a drop-down list
I can use conditional formatting to highlight information
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: format · model · formula · COUNTIF · data validation · conditional format. TA: the RSC Live event seating spreadsheet open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — who uses spreadsheets? Pupils SORT tasks under teacher / shopkeeper / event planner.
   • Support: what data validation / a drop-down does.  • Core: why a drop-down beats typing.  • Challenge: a useful rule for a sold seat.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — prices from one place. Worked example =AB6*25% for students, =AB6*75% for over 60s; change one cell, all update.
3. (20–35) COUNTIF. =COUNTIF(H8:Q9,"A") counts Premier adult seats. Pupils drag the COUNTIF parts into order (Parsons), then match seat codes (Support), write seats-remaining + fill-blank speech marks (Core). Likely error: missing speech marks → fix-words “text needs speech marks.”
4. (35–50) You-do — data validation + conditional formatting. Add a drop-down list of seat codes; colour sold seats with an “is not empty” rule; explain how validation keeps the totals correct (Challenge). Screenshot onto the activity worksheet. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil shows a drop-down working.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: RSC merchandise — profit model and conditional formatting
lessons.objectives:
I can calculate profit for each item with a formula
I can total a column with SUM
I can format money cells as currency
I can use conditional formatting to show if a target is met
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: format · model · formula · calculate percentage · conditional format. TA: the RSC merchandise spreadsheet open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–15) Starter — there’s always something to buy. Pupils SORT merch into cheaper / more expensive items.
   • Support: what conditional formatting / currency is.  • Core: name the mark-up.  • Challenge: describe a rule to turn G5 green at target.
2. (15–25) I-do → we-do — wholesale vs retail; the mark-up is the profit. Worked example =(C8-B8)*D8 read in cell order. Likely error: missing brackets → fix-words “brackets first.”
3. (25–40) You-do — the model. Drag the profit formula parts into order (Parsons), choose the right format (Support), total with SUM + fill-blank (Core), write the row-8 profit formula. Then format money as currency.
4. (40–50) Conditional formatting — set a rule so forecast profit shows red below target / green at target (Challenge). Screenshot onto the activity worksheet. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil shows their profit total.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: RSC data visualisation — charts, titles and labels
lessons.objectives:
I can make a column chart
I can make a pie chart
I can give a chart a clear title and labels
I can choose the right chart for the data
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: chart · title · label · legend · axis. TA: the completed Lesson 1 and 2 spreadsheets open (or the completed copies). Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–15) Starter — charts can mislead. Pupils critique a chart whose vertical axis does not start at zero.
   • Support: a chart needs a title / pie shows parts of a whole.  • Core: why a truncated axis exaggerates.  • Challenge: two ways a chart misleads.
2. (15–25) I-do → we-do — column vs pie. Column compares amounts; pie shows parts of a whole. Pupils SORT jobs to the right chart and ORDER the steps to make a chart.
3. (25–40) You-do — make a column chart of votes and a pie chart of seat sales; give clear titles and labels; move each to its own sheet. Likely error: a vague/missing title → fix-words “the title says what the data is.”
4. (40–50) Right chart for the job — a pie is the wrong choice to compare separate groups; use a column chart (Challenge). Screenshot one chart onto the activity worksheet. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil shows a chart and says why they chose it.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: RSC stock control — LOOKUP and IF functions
lessons.objectives:
I can use VLOOKUP to fetch item data
I can lock a lookup range with $ signs
I can use an IF function to warn when stock is low
I can test a stock control model
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: LOOKUP · VLOOKUP · IF · absolute · conditional format. TA: the RSC merchandise database spreadsheet open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–15) Starter — what happens after the beep? A barcode holds item data, not the price; the price lives in the database.
   • Support: what LOOKUP / IF does.  • Core: an advantage of price-in-database.  • Challenge: what a QR code stores that a barcode cannot.
2. (15–25) I-do → we-do — a till uses a LOOKUP. =VLOOKUP(A8,'Lookup sheet'!$A$2:$C$17,3,FALSE): A8 = item number, locked range, 3 = third column, FALSE = exact match.
3. (25–40) You-do — the lookup. Drag the VLOOKUP parts into order (Parsons), choose what A8/FALSE mean (Support), then an IF to warn on low stock with fill-blank (Core). Likely error: an unlocked range that shifts when filled down → fix-words “lock the range with dollar signs.”
4. (40–50) Test it — enter an item number, sell items until “Reorder” appears, write a CHECK INPUT IF (Challenge). Screenshot a working lookup onto the activity worksheet. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil shows a lookup that worked.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Spreadsheets assessment — end-of-unit quiz
lessons.objectives:
I can apply my spreadsheet skills to a new scenario
I can write formulae from scratch
I can explain what a computer model is
I can choose the right function for a task
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: model · formula · function · format. TA: the assessment (fete) spreadsheet open; the end-of-unit quiz worksheet ready. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — read the formula. Pupils say what =SUM(D8:D22) does, then MATCH each function to its job (Support), explain a computer model (Core), give a real-life model use (Challenge). The starter carries today’s differentiation.
2. (10–40) Assessment — the end-of-unit quiz worksheet (the folded summative). A new summer-fete spreadsheet: write formulae (D9, D24, J8, J15 as code), explain formats and a computer model (text), choose the right function (single-choice AVERAGE/COUNT/IF). Exam conditions. Fix-words on the board: “read the formula in cell order; lock with $ where a cell must stay.”
3. (40–50) Finish + screenshot — calm independent work; look back at earlier lessons if stuck. Screenshot the finished spreadsheet onto the quiz worksheet. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (50–55) End of unit — recap the journey: basics → formulae → formatting → models, charts, lookups and IF. Self-tick the “I can…”.
Differentiation: the starter is on the SAME task at three depths; the quiz is a shared assessment everyone sits. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
